data.title: Citi strategist sharply cuts S&P 500 target
data.description:
Citi equity strategist Scott Chronert cut his year-end target for U.S. large-cap stocks in the face of uncertainty caused by tariff policy.

Chronert cut his S&P 500 (SP500) (NYSEARCA:SPY) (NYSEARCA:IVV) (NYSEARCA:VOO) target to 5,800 for the end of 2025 from 6,500. He also cut his EPS forecast for the index as a whole to $255 from $270.

“No doubt, the goldilocks sentiment in place entering this year has given way to abject uncertainty,” Chronert said.

The trade system in place since China joined the WTO in 2001 will be disrupted by the White House tariff approach, he added.

The move comes as Citi's global equity team cut the U.S. market to Neutral from Outperform, saying the cases for "exceptionalism" are fading and tariffs could damage U.S. EPS the most.

data.title: Japanese regulator issues cease and desist order to Google
data.description:
On Tuesday, the Japan Fair Trade Commission issued a cease and desist order against Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) Google for alleged anticompetitive practices.

The Japanese competition watchdog noted that Google violated some provisions related to unfair trade practices under the Antimonopoly Act. The JFTC added that it published the start of the investigation on October 23rd, 2023. The regulator noted that, during the probe, it exchanged information with overseas competition authorities that investigated Google's act similar to this case.

The JFTC was looking into Google's practice of asking Android smartphone makers to prioritize its search services on their devices.

Google did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

The regulator said that Google required smartphone makers to preinstall Google Search and place its widget and icon (including a folder storing the icon) on the Default Home Screen; and to preinstall Google Chrome, place its icon on the Default Home Screen, and not to change the setting of Google Chrome in which Google's Search Functionality is selected.

The JFTC also said that Google allegedly offered to share ad revenue with device makers if they agreed not to preload search engines from third parties (excluding users). The agency noted that Google wanted its General Search Services for all Search Functionality.

data.title: Applied Digital stock falls after Q3 sales miss
data.description:
Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) shares fell over 11% in premarket trade on Tuesday after the digital infrastructure company reported third quarter revenue that fell short of market expectations.

The company incurred adjusted loss per share of -$0.08 on revenue of $52.9M, up 22% from the prior year comparable period. Analysts had expected the company to post losses of -$0.11/share on $63.4M revenue.

Applied Digital's data center hosting business recorded a 7% decline in revenue to $35.2M, while its cloud services business generated $17.8M in revenues compared to $5.6M in the prior year period.

The management said during the post-earnings call that board has decided to explore strategic options for the cloud services business, aiming to reduce friction with potential data center customers and capitalize on favorable market conditions, as the company looks to transition into a data center real estate investment trust.

data.title: Futures tick up as traders continue to play 'guess-the-tariffs'
data.description:
Stock index futures were slightly higher Tuesday after a modest gain to start the week.

Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +0.4%, S&P futures (SPX) +0.2% and Dow futures (INDU) +0.2% were slightly higher.

"With the latest advance for equities, that now means that the S&P 500 has recovered to -4.67% beneath its level at the tariff announcement, and -12.01% beneath its mid-February peak," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said. "That’s clearly a significant decline, but it’s still some distance from a normal recession decline, or even some of the larger non-recession bear markets of recent years like 2022, which saw a -25% peak-to-trough fall. So it’s clear that investors aren’t convinced that a recession is inevitable just yet."

Investors were still trying to gauge what kinds of tariff landscape the global economy will face. 

President Donald Trump has hinted at a reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, while the administration has begun investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors.

Trump "signaled a possible retreat from auto tariffs to allow companies to relocate production to the US," UBS chief economist Paul Donovan said. "There are some challenges to this thinking. Relocation would not be quick. Relocation would add costs (unnecessarily duplicating factories). Relocation would require long-term investment in an erratic policy environment."

Treasury yields were slightly higher after recent declines. The 10-year yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.38%.

On the economic calendar, the April Empire State Manufacturing Index arrives before the bell. The forecast is for a rise to -12.80 from -20.

"We think a sharp hit to business confidence from President Trump’s shock tariff announcement means the headline index will follow the 25.7-point plunge in March with a further small decline in April," Pantheon Macro said. "We look for a dip to minus 25, from minus 20."

At the same time, March import/export price numbers hit. Both are expected to have risen 0.1% on the month.

"US March import and export price data does not show the inflation from tariffs (that is applied after the import prices are calculated)," Donovan said. "Monitoring the trends in import price data should confirm that the tariff tax burden is paid by the US, as is generally the case."

data.title: WildBrain gives update on TV channel sales
Sterling Infrastructure to replace Patterson Companies in S&P SmallCap 600
PrveCeutical upsizes private placement
data.description:
PreveCeutical Medical (OTCQB:PRVCF) increased the size of its previously announced non-brokered private placement from $500K to $700K, resulting in up to 23.33M units at a price of $0.03 per unit.

Each unit will be comprised of one common share and one-half share purchase warrant.

The company intends to use the aggregate gross proceeds from the sale of the offering to pay outstanding payables, for operating expenses, and for general working capital purposes.

The closing of the offering is subject to receipt of all necessary regulatory approvals, including from the CSE.
